mustHaveElements: Landscape as antagonist — the West must be indifferent, vast, and physically consequential
Elegiac register — awareness that the open-range era is ending
Specificity of work — cattle drives, horse-breaking, fence-building, cooking, farriery
Violence with consequence — gunshots leave wounds that fester, kill people who are mourned
Moral ambiguity — heroes who have done unforgivable things, villains who were once children
Community as ecosystem — the town/camp/ranch as living social organism
Humor — dry, cruel, or absurd, baked into character not separate buffoonery
dealBreakers: Anachronistic values — 21st-century moral framing without dramatic friction
Landscape as wallpaper — described once and forgotten
The Magical Native — indigenous characters as wisdom dispensers only
Revenge as the only engine — a man with a gun hunting wrongdoers is B-movie material
No women or women as prizes — historically inaccurate and dramatically impoverished
The Stoic Hero who doesn't change — a narrative dead end
Sentimental ending — the ride into the sunset undoes the elegy
canonicalComparables: Lonesome Dove (McMurtry) — gold standard for ensemble depth, tonal range, elegiac register
Blood Meridian (McCarthy) — landscape as moral vacuum, beauty and horror in same sentence
The Searchers (LeMay/Ford) — revenge quest revealing revenger's own darkness
True Grit (Portis) — distinctive voice refreshing tired conventions
Deadwood (Milch) — building community under siege, language as character

essentialRoles.role: The Moral Anchor with a Past
essentialRoles.function: Ethical center carrying enough guilt for complicated judgments
Generates humor, articulates thematic questions, irreverent wisdom
essentialRoles.avoidance: Comic relief who only tells jokes

essentialRoles.function: Embodies old order passing, competent but carrying failure

essentialRoles.role: The Woman Who Refuses Her Role
essentialRoles.function: Perspective on masculine codes, agency within constraints
Drives action through poor decisions, grows or dies
Entry point for reader, lens for critique without authorial intrusion

essentialRoles.role: The Antagonist with a Competing Vision

recommendedEnsembleSize.tertiary: World-populating background characters with implied off-page lives

diversityRequirements: Women with agency beyond domestic/romantic roles — ranch owners, madams, doctors, teachers
Indigenous perspectives as full subjects — Comanche, Apache, Lakota with politics and interiority
Mexican/Tejano characters as community members — the West was Mexican territory first
Black cowboys and Buffalo Soldiers — one in four cowboys was Black
Class diversity — land barons through homesteaders through outlaws
Age range — children through elderly; a world without children has no stakes for the future

effectivePatterns: The Journey as Revelation — herd drive/pursuit/migration stripping characters of pretense
The Community Under Pressure — town/outpost facing external threat with internal fractures
The Double-Linear — two narrative threads commenting on each other thematically
The Siege Economy — limited resources forcing escalating moral choices
The Moral Reckoning — past sin resurfacing and demanding payment
Seasonal Structure — spring roundup through winter survival, using land's rhythms
avoidPatterns: The Treasure Map — buried gold / lost mine driving plot by greed
The Lone Avenger — one man against corrupt town fits TV episode not novel
The Railroad as Unambiguous Villain — must be represented by specific people
The White Savior — white protagonist resolving indigenous/Mexican conflicts
Episodic without accumulation — adventures that don't change characters
pacingPrinciples: Alternate tension/respite in deliberate rhythm — four-beat cycle: comic → threat → violent → elegiac
Use landscape description as pacing mechanism — force reader to slow down and feel scale
Sprawl is a feature — every character needs room to be wrong, change their mind, disappoint
Subplots intersect thematically not mechanically — trajectories commenting on each other
Let minor characters have their own climaxes — each character's story arrives somewhere

ldTonalRegisters: Rough comedy — teasing, absurdity of men cooking, warmth behind mockery
Unadorned violence — fast, confusing, ugly, spare prose
Elegiac reflection — awareness of loss, landscape as carrier of grief
Tender domesticity — meals eaten together, horses gentled, children put to bed
Philosophical rumination — characters articulating worldview badly, ideas feeling discovered
Dread — atmospheric foreboding through weather, landscape, offhand remarks
commonTonalErrors: Grimdark without relief — violence and misery with no beauty or humor
Nostalgic sentimentality — 'good old days' framing turning West into theme park
Modern irony — winking tone that signals author is above the material
Purple landscape prose — 'golden rays kissed crimson mesas' instead of precision
Maundering pace — slow scenes that don't reveal character or advance emotional logic

decisions.decision: Set in the 1880s to maximize elegiac register — fences going up, Indian Wars concluding, buffalo near extinction
decisions.rationale: The elegy requires historical specificity; the 1880s is the open range's last decade

decisions.decision: Protagonist should be rancher/drover/horse trader, not gunslinger or lawman — story about making a living, not making a name
decisions.rationale: LD's best work is about labor; texture, stakes, and built-in clock

decisions.decision: Include co-equal woman's POV with own arc, stakes, and climax
decisions.rationale: Single greatest Western weakness is marginalization of women; historically honest and narratively strengthening

decisions.decision: Antagonist must have legitimate grievance — worldview reader can't entirely dismiss
decisions.rationale: Judge Holden works because philosophically compelling; Blue Duck works because hatred is earned

decisions.decision: Kill at least one primary character before final act — death should feel wasteful, random, unbearably sad
decisions.rationale: Establishes world doesn't care about narrative justice; reader is braced for later losses

decisions.decision: Structure around two co-equal male protagonists with opposing philosophies bound by shared history
decisions.rationale: The Call/Gus dynamic generates plot, theme, emotional weight more efficiently than any other structural choice

successCriteriaRecommendations.metric: Every primary character has at least one scene acting against expectation that forces reader re-evaluation
successCriteriaRecommendations.rationale: The moment of contradiction IS the character

successCriteriaRecommendations.metric: Landscape directly causes or prevents at least 3 significant plot events
successCriteriaRecommendations.rationale: If plot is portable, Western is cosmetic

successCriteriaRecommendations.metric: At least one scene of unguarded tenderness between non-romantically-linked characters
successCriteriaRecommendations.rationale: Makes violence hurt; proves author understands love not just violence

successCriteriaRecommendations.metric: Closing 10% must contain emotional loss NOT redeemed by ending — lower key than beginning
successCriteriaRecommendations.threshold: Final chapter must not feel like victory

successCriteriaRecommendations.metric: At least 3 women with distinct internal lives independent of male characters
successCriteriaRecommendations.rationale: Western canon weakest here; exceeding bar earns place

successCriteriaRecommendations.metric: Protagonist fails someone they love through act of commission or omission — failure never fully forgiven
successCriteriaRecommendations.rationale: Protagonist must be damaged enough that redemption is impossible

ranking: The elegy — awareness world is ending baked into premise
Historical verisimilitude — specificity as engine of authenticity
Tonal range — comedy and tenderness are not optional
Moral complexity — no pure heroes, no pure villains
Violence with gravity — never cheap, never entertaining
Diverse perspectives as narrative strength not obligation
Originality within the tradition — going deeper not sideways
summary: This Western world will be exceptional if it borrows Lonesome Dove's deepest lesson: the genre is not about cowboys or gunfights but about the end of things — a way of life, a set of relationships, an idea of freedom that was unsustainable but beautiful while it lasted. The characters must feel like people you could meet, not types you recognize. The landscape must be a presence that demands responses, not a postcard. The violence must hurt. The comedy must be earned. And the ending must leave the reader with the specific ache of something passing that cannot be brought back. It will be forgettable if it hits the expected beats without understanding what they mean; if it treats the West as a setting rather than a subject; if its characters are functions rather than people; or if it lacks the courage to make the reader sad. The bar is the Pulitzer. Do not aim lower.
